stocks.name: Labor
stocks.comment: The labor force of the firm.

stocks.name: Vacancies
stocks.comment: The number of open positions the firm seeks to fill.

stocks.name: Pink Noise in Prody
stocks.comment:
Pink Noise is first-order autocorrelated noise.  Pink noise provides a realistic  noise input to
		        models in which the next random shock depends in part on the previous  shocks.  The user
		        can specify the correlation time.  The mean and standard deviation are  specified
		         by the user.

stocks.name: Inventory
stocks.comment:
The level of finished goods inventory in the plant.  Increased by production and  decreased by
		         shipments.  Initially set to the desired inventory level.

stocks.name: Expected Order Rate
stocks.comment:
The demand forecast is formed by adaptive expectations, using exponential smoothing,  a common
		         forecasting technique.  The initial forecast is equal to the  initial customer order rate.

stocks.name: Work in Process Inventory
stocks.comment: WIP inventory accumulates the difference between production starts and  completions.

stocks.name: Pink Noise
stocks.comment:
Pink Noise is first-order autocorrelated noise.  Pink noise provides a realistic  noise input to
		        models in which the next random shock depends in part on the previous  shocks.  The user
		        can specify the correlation time.  The mean is 0 and the standard deviation  is specified
		         by the user.

flows.name: Pink Noise in Prody net flow
flows.comment: Net flow for Pink Noise in Prody

flows.name: Expected Order Rate net flow
flows.comment: Net flow for Expected Order Rate

variables.name: Adjustment for Labor
variables.comment: Adjusts the desired hiring rate to bring labor in line with the desired  level.

variables.name: Production Start Rate
variables.comment: The production start rate is determined by the labor force,  productivity,  and the workweek.

variables.name: Adjustment for Vacancies
variables.comment: Adjusts vacancy creation so the firm has the desired number of vacancies.

variables.name: Layoff Rate
variables.comment: The layoff rate is the lesser of the desired or maximum rate.

variables.name: Average Duration of Employment
variables.comment: The average duration of employment is constant in this simple model, but  in general depends on labor market and economic conditions, as well as  employee satisfaction and other factors internal to the firm.

variables.name: Average Layoff Time
variables.comment: The average time required to lay off a worker.

variables.name: Hiring Rate
variables.comment: Hiring depends on the number of open vacancies and the average time  required to fill a vacancy.

variables.name: Average Time to Fill Vacancies
variables.comment: The average time required to fill a vacancy and bring a new employee into  the workforce.

variables.name: Quit Rate
variables.comment: The rate at which employees voluntarily leave the firm.  Determined by the  average duration of employment.

variables.name: Desired Vacancy Cancellation Rate
variables.comment: The desired rate of vacancy cancellation, given by the desired vacancy  creation rate whenever that rate is negative.

variables.name: Time to Form Expected Productivity
variables.comment: The average time required for managers to revise their estimates of labor  productivity.

variables.name: Desired Hiring Rate
variables.comment: The firm seeks to hire enough people to replace expected attrition and  adjust the labor force to the desired level.

variables.name: Maximum Vacancy Cancellation Rate
variables.comment: The maximum vacancy cancellation rate is determined by the numer of  vacancies outstanding and the minimum cancellation time.

variables.name: Vacancy Adjustment Time
variables.comment: The average time over which the firm attempts to adjust the actual number  of vacancies to the desired level.

variables.name: Desired Labor
variables.comment: Desired labor is given by the desired production start rate, expected  productivity per hour, and the planned workweek.

variables.name: Desired Lay Off Rate
variables.comment: The firm desires to lay people off whenever the desired hiring rate is  negative, modified by their willingness to lay off.  Willingness to Lay  Off = 0 implies a no layoff policy.

variables.name: Vacancy Creation Rate
variables.comment: The rate at which the firm authorizes new positions and begins to recruit  for them.

variables.name: Expected Attrition Rate
variables.comment: In this simple model expected attrition is set to the actual voluntary  quit rate.

variables.name: Desired Vacancies
variables.comment: The number of vacancies needed to generate the desired hiring rate, given  the expected time required to fill a vacancy.

variables.name: Vacancy Cancellation Time
variables.comment: The average time required to cancel a vacancy.

variables.name: Desired Vacancy Creation Rate
variables.comment: The firm seeks to create enough vacancies to yield the desired hiring  rate, adjusted to bring the stock of vacancies in line with the desired  stock.

variables.name: Vacancy Closure Rate
variables.comment: Hiring a new employee fills a vacancy.

variables.name: Willingness to Lay Off
variables.comment: The willingness to layoff employees.  Zero = no layoff policy; 1 implies  the firm is just as willing to layoff employees when there are too many as  to hire when there are too few.

variables.name: Labor Adjustment Time
variables.comment: The time period over which the firm seeks to bring the labor force in line  with the desired level.

variables.name: Maximum Layoff Rate
variables.comment: The maximum layoff rate is determined by the number of workers and the  layoff time.

variables.name: Expected Time to Fill Vacancies
variables.comment: The expected time required to fill vacancies.  In this simple model, set  to the actual time.  A better formulation would use an information delay  to represent the delay in gathering information about hiring delays and  changing procedures.

variables.name: Vacancy Cancellation Rate
variables.comment: The rate at which the firm cancels existing vacancies prior to filling  them.

variables.name: Normally Distributed Noise
variables.comment: A white noise signal, distributed normally, with a mean and standard  deviation set by the modeler.  The distribution is truncated 0²Noise²4;  the lower limit maintains nonnegativity of productivity; the upper limit  is high relative to plausible standard deviations.  The noise seen can  also be varied.

variables.name: Expected Productivity
variables.comment: Expected productivity is the firm's belief about the productivity of the  labor force, and adjusts to actual productivity with a delay.  First order  smoothing is assumed.

variables.name: Start Time for Noise in Productivity
variables.comment: The time at which the random variations in productivity begin.

variables.name: Average Productivity
variables.comment: The average productivity of the workforce

variables.name: Mean for Random Effects on Productivity
variables.comment: The mean of the random effects.  Set to unity because the random effects  multiply average productivity.

variables.name: Switch for Pink Noise in Productivity
variables.comment: Switch to choose between pink noise or normally distributed white noise  for labor productivity

variables.name: Change in Pink Noise in Prody
variables.comment:
Change in the pink noise value; Pink noise is a first order exponential smoothing  delay of the white
		         noise input.

variables.name: Productivity
variables.comment: Productivity is average productivity perturbed by a random variable  representing unmodeled sources of variation in output per person.

variables.name: Random Effects on Productivity
variables.comment: The random effects on productivity can be set either to normally  distributed white noise or pink noise.  Noise begins at the noise start  time.

variables.name: Random Seed for Productivity
variables.comment: Changing the random number seed changes the sequence of values generated  by the random process.

variables.name: Standard Deviation in Random Effects on Productivity
variables.comment: The standard deviation of the random variations in productivity.

variables.name: White Noise in Prody
variables.comment: White noise input to the pink noise process.

variables.name: Noise Correlation Time for Productivity
variables.comment: The correlation time constant for Pink Noise.

variables.name: Switch for Labor in Production
variables.comment: Switch = 1 implies production starts are determined by the labor force.   When Switch = 0, production starts equal desired production starts.

variables.name: Standard Workweek
variables.comment: The firm's target for the workweek.

variables.name: Desired Production Start Rate
variables.comment:
The desired rate of production starts, equal to the desired production rate adjusted  by the adequacy
		         of the WIP inventory.

variables.name: Workweek
variables.comment: The average workweek.

variables.name: Ratio of Maximum to Desired Shipments
variables.comment: The ratio of the maximum to desired shipment rate.  Ratios less than one  indicate shipments are constrained below the desired level.

variables.name: Order Fulfillment Ratio
variables.comment: The Fraction of customer orders filled is determined by the ratio of the  normal shipment rate to the desired rate.  The normal rate is the rate  current inventory permits under normal circumstances.  Low inventory  availability reduces shipments below customer orders.  Unfilled customer  orders are lost.

variables.name: Shipment Rate
variables.comment: The shipment rate is the desired shipment rate multiplied by the fraction  of orders filled (the order fulfillment ratio.   Shipments fall below  desired shipments when the feasible shipment rate falls below the desired  rate, indicating that some products are unavailable.

variables.name: Desired Shipment Rate
variables.comment: The desired shipment rate equals the customer order rate.  In this model  there is no backlog of unfilled orders:  unfilled orders are lost as  customers seek alternate sources of supply.

variables.name: Inventory Coverage
variables.comment: Inventory coverage is given by the ratio of inventory to shipments.

variables.name: Desired Inventory Coverage
variables.comment:
Desired inventory coverage is the number of weeks of the demand forecast the plant  seeks to maintain
		        in inventory.  This inventory coverage is required to maintain delivery  reliability by
		         buffering the plant against unforeseen variations in demand or  production.  It consists of the normal order processing time plus an  additional term representing the coverage desired to maintain safety  stocks.

variables.name: Maximum Shipment Rate
variables.comment: The maximum rate of shipments the firm can achieve given their current  inventory level and the minimum order processing time.

variables.name: Minimum Order Processing Time
variables.comment: The minimum time required to process and ship an order.

variables.name: Safety Stock Coverage
variables.comment: Safety stock coverage is the number of weeks of the expected order rate  the firm would like to maintain in inventory over and above the normal  order processing time.  The safety stock provides a buffer against the  possibility that unforeseen variations in demand will cause shipments to  fall below orders.

variables.name: Desired Inventory
variables.comment:
The desired inventory level sought by the plant.  Experience suggests that to  maintain customer
		        service by providing full and reliable deliveries, the plant must maintain a  certain
		         coverage of throughput (demand), estimated by the demand forecast.

variables.name: Change in Exp Orders
variables.comment:
The demand forecast adjusts to the actual order rate over a time period determined  by the Time to
		        Average Order Rate.  The demand forecast is formed by first-order  exponential smoothing,
		         a widely used forecasting technique.

variables.name: Time to Average Order Rate
variables.comment: The demand forecast adjusts to actual customer orders over this time  period.

variables.name: Production Rate
variables.comment:
Production is a third order delay of the production start rate, with the delay time  determined by
		         the manufacturing cycle time.

variables.name: Manufacturing Cycle Time
variables.comment: The average delay between the start and completion of production

variables.name: Adjustment for WIP
variables.comment: The adjustment to the production start rate from the adequacy of WIP  inventory.

variables.name: WIP Adjustment Time
variables.comment: The time required to adjust the WIP inventory to the desired level.

variables.name: Desired WIP
variables.comment:
The desired quantity of work in process inventory.  Proportional to the  manufacturing cycle time and
		         the desired rate of production.

variables.name: Desired Production
variables.comment:
Desired Production is the demand forecast (Expected Order Rate) adjusted to bring  the inventory
		         position in line with the target inventory level.

variables.name: Production Adjustment from Inventory
variables.comment:
The desired production rate is adjusted above or below the forecast based on the  inventory position
		        of the plant.  When desired inventory > inventory, desired production is  increased (and
		         vice-versa).  Inventory gaps are corrected over the inv. adj.  time.

variables.name: Inventory Adjustment Time
variables.comment:
The inventory adjustment time is the time period over which the plant seeks to bring  inventory in
		         balance with the desired level.  Initially set to 12 weeks.

variables.name: Customer Order Rate
variables.comment:
Customer order rate is exogenous.  A variety of test inputs allow users to try  different patterns,
		         including a step, pulse, sine wave, and random noise.

variables.name: Initial Customer Order Rate
variables.comment: Initial value of customer orders, set to 10,000 widgets per week.

variables.name: Input
variables.comment:
Input is a dimensionless variable which provides a variety of test input patterns,  including a step,
		         pulse, sine wave, and random noise.

variables.name: Step Height
variables.comment: Height of step input to customer orders, as fraction of initial value.

variables.name: Step Time
variables.comment: Time for the step input.

variables.name: Pulse Quantity
variables.comment:
The quantity to be injected to customer orders, as a fraction of the base value of  Input. 
		        For example, to pulse in a quantity equal to 50% of the current value of  input, set to
		         .50.

variables.name: Pulse Time
variables.comment: Time at which the pulse in Input occurs.

variables.name: Ramp Slope
variables.comment: Slope of the ramp input, as a fraction of the base value (per week).

variables.name: Ramp Start Time
variables.comment: Start time for the ramp input.

variables.name: Ramp End Time
variables.comment: End time for the ramp input.

variables.name: Sine Amplitude
variables.comment: Amplitude of sine wave in customer orders (fraction of mean).

variables.name: Sine Period
variables.comment: Period of sine wave in customer demand.  Set initially to 50 weeks (1  year).

variables.name: Noise Start Time
variables.comment: Start time for the random input.

variables.name: Change in Pink Noise
variables.comment:
Change in the pink noise value; Pink noise is a first order exponential smoothing  delay of the white
		         noise input.

variables.name: Noise Correlation Time
variables.comment: The correlation time constant for Pink Noise.

variables.name: White Noise
variables.comment: White noise input to the pink noise process.

variables.name: Noise Standard Deviation
variables.comment: The standard deviation of the pink noise process.

variables.name: Noise Seed
variables.comment: Random number generator seed.  Vary to generate a different sequence of  random numbers.

lookupTables.name: Table for Order Fulfillment
lookupTables.comment:
The ability to ship is constrained by inventory availability.  As the inventory  level drops, the 
		        fraction of customer orders that can be filled decreases.  When inventory is  zero, shipments
		         cease.  Unfilled customer orders are lost.\!\!\!

Standard Deviation in Random Effects on Productivity
Switch for Pink Noise in Productivity
Mean for Random Effects on Productivity

metadata.author: Dr. Erik Pruyt
metadata.source: Pruyt, E., 2013. Small System Dynamics Models for Big Issues: Triple Jump towards Real-World Complexity. TU Delft Library. ISBN 978-94-6186-195-5
